title: Small Business Website Design in Australia
meta_title: Small Business Website Design Australia | OptiTide
meta_description: Small business website design in Australia from $750 one-off, GST inclusive, no lock-in contracts. What your site really needs, what to skip, and real costs.
intro: If you run a small business in Australia and need a website, the hard part usually is not the building. It is working out what you actually need, what it should reasonably cost, and what you can safely ignore.
body_html:
What a small business website actually needs

A small business website has one job: to convince someone who has already heard of you that you are worth contacting. That person arrives from a Google search, a social post or a recommendation, and they decide quickly, often before they have read past the first screen, whether to call you, email you, or go back to the results page.

That job needs less than people expect. A plain statement of what you do and who you do it for, visible without scrolling. Your services described in the words customers use themselves rather than industry language. Some indication of price, or at least of the range, if your business is able to publish one. Signals that you are a real operation, such as an ABN, a business email address and a contact form that actually sends. And one obvious way to get in touch, repeated on every page.

It also has to work properly on a phone. A layout that only behaves on a desktop monitor loses the visitor who tapped through from a search result on the train, and a page weighed down with oversized images loses the one on a patchy suburban connection. Those two things are worth more than any visual flourish you could add.

What it does not need

Not everything sold alongside a website earns its place. Depending on your business, you can often skip:

  • A rotating homepage banner. Slide two is only ever seen by a visitor who waits for it, and the extra images slow the page down for everyone who does not.
  • A blog you have no intention of writing. An empty blog with three posts from two years ago reads worse than no blog at all.
  • Custom booking, quoting or portal features built before you know whether customers will use them. Start with a form and an email address, then build the tool once the demand is proven.
  • Stock photography of strangers in a boardroom. Photos of your real work, your staff or your premises show a visitor something a stock library cannot, even taken on a phone.
  • Twenty pages when five would do. Every extra page is another thing to keep accurate.

What small business website design costs in Australia

Prices vary enormously, which makes the market a confusing one to shop in. Ours are published so you can hold them against anything else you are quoted. The Starter package is $750 one-off and the Business package is $1,500 one-off, both GST inclusive, and larger or more complicated builds are quoted individually. There is no lock-in contract. The detail sits on the web design service page.

Those prices are GST inclusive, so the figures above already have the tax inside them rather than adding ten per cent at the end. Invoices are issued through your client portal, and the payment methods accepted are PayID and bank transfer, Skrill, PayPal and Payoneer.

The costs that keep running

A website is not a single purchase, so budget separately for the parts that recur. You will need a domain name renewed each year. You will need hosting, which with us is $25 per month unmanaged or $50 per month managed; how the two tiers differ is set out on the hosting page, and it is worth asking which one suits your site before you commit to either. Getting found for searches you do not currently rank for is a separate ongoing job again: SEO is $750, $1,500 or $2,500 per month depending on the tier, and social media marketing is $250 per month. All of those are GST inclusive with no lock-in contract, so you can stop when it stops being worth it to you.

Why the cheap option often costs more later

Do-it-yourself builders are genuinely good value for some businesses. If you have the time, a simple offering and no plan to compete in search results, a template site you assemble yourself can be the right call. Just be honest with yourself about the time, because the hours spent fighting a page builder are hours not spent on paid work.

The expensive failures in this industry tend to follow a familiar shape. A site gets built by whoever was cheapest, on a platform or page builder nobody else wants to touch. Nothing is documented. The person who built it stops answering emails. Two years later a phone number needs changing on eight pages, or the contact form has quietly stopped delivering and the first hint of it is a customer who assumed they were being ignored. At that point you are not paying someone to update a website. You are paying them to work out how the old one was put together first, and often to rebuild it anyway.

The things that prevent that outcome are unglamorous. Own your domain name, registered in your business name, with the login in your own possession. Make sure you can get a copy of the site and its content whenever you ask for it. Know who to contact when something breaks. And ask any developer, including us, what happens if you decide to leave.

How we work

OptiTide is Michael James Long trading as OptiTide, ABN 38 163 865 712, Australian owned and operated, registered in Victoria. There is no office to visit, in Perth or anywhere else, and what matters more than an address is the clock: hours are Monday to Friday, 9 AM to 9 PM AWST, which is Perth time. The late finish is the point of the arrangement rather than an accident of it: whether you are in Perth, Sydney, Melbourne or Brisbane, feedback you send at the end of your working day still lands inside ours, and what we have done with it is waiting for you the next morning.

All the work is done remotely and we work with clients across Australia. You get a client portal where you can view and pay your invoices, track your project and message us, so the details that matter are not buried somewhere in an email thread. If you want the process in full, read how we work, look over the other services, or get in touch with a short description of your business and what you need.

faqs.q: How much does a small business website cost in Australia?
faqs.a: The Starter website is $750 as a one-off and the Business package is $1,500 as a one-off, both GST inclusive with no lock-in contract. Larger or more complicated builds are quoted individually. Budget separately for a domain name renewed each year and for hosting, which is $25 per month unmanaged or $50 per month managed.

faqs.q: Which package should I choose for a first website?
faqs.a: Starter is $750 and Business is $1,500, both one-off and both GST inclusive. Which one fits depends on how much you have to explain and how much of it is already written down, so send an outline of your business through the contact form and we will confirm the tier and what it covers before you pay anything.

faqs.q: Do I have to host the website with OptiTide?
faqs.a: Hosting is listed and billed as its own service rather than bundled into the build price: $25 per month unmanaged or $50 per month managed, GST inclusive, with no lock-in contract. Which of the two suits your site, or whether you would rather host somewhere else, is worth raising before the build starts so the arrangement can be confirmed in writing.

faqs.q: Are you based in Perth, and do I need to be?
faqs.a: No to both. We are not based in Perth: OptiTide is registered in Victoria, the work is done remotely and we take clients right across Australia. What is genuinely Perth about us is the clock. Hours are Monday to Friday, 9 AM to 9 PM AWST, so a WA client deals with someone on their own time, and the deliberately late finish means an east-coast client can send feedback at the end of their day and review the result the next morning.

faqs.q: What payment methods do you accept?
faqs.a: PayID and bank transfer, Skrill, PayPal and Payoneer. Invoices are issued through your client portal, where you can view them, pay them and keep a record of what has already been paid. All prices are GST inclusive, so the quoted figure already has GST inside it rather than adding ten per cent at the end.
